skill.name: ai-collaborate-teaching
skill.description: Design AI-assisted programming lessons that balance foundational practice, guided AI collaboration, ethical use, and independent verification.
skill.summary: Create balanced AI collaboration lessons for programming education.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found them in Markdown examples, templates, and educational prose. No evidence found of malicious intent, command execution, prompt injection, credential access, or outbound data exfiltration. The only confirmed operational risk is local file reading by helper scripts for user-supplied lesson and prompt files.

low_findings.title: Local File Parsing Helper Scripts
low_findings.description: The helper scripts read files from paths supplied by the user and print assessment results. This is expected for lesson and prompt validation tools, but users should run them only on files they intend to analyze.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly open user-specified files and parse local YAML or text. No network transfer, shell execution, or secret access was found in the reviewed script context.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Static Hits Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported command-execution locations are fenced examples, prompt templates, and instructional dialogue. They are not executable code paths and do not invoke shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown or YAML-style examples inside documentation. They contain no runtime shell APIs or dynamic execution flow.

low_findings.title: Network Static Hits Are Security Teaching Examples
low_findings.description: The Python HTTP library references appear in a lesson about AI suggesting outdated or unsafe code. The same example recommends secure verification and is not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reference is a Markdown example contrasting unsafe and safer requests usage. No imported HTTP client or runtime network call was found in executable files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Hits Are Instructional Text
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto and reconnaissance patterns are words in pedagogy materials, examples, and checklist content. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation or reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are educational descriptions about AI use, security-critical topics, or template headings. They do not contain executable cryptographic code, scanning logic, or system probing.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation Static Hit Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The obfuscation finding points to the start of a normal Python validator script. The file uses standard imports, functions, type hints, and JSON output rather than obfuscated code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged file begins with a shebang and a descriptive module docstring. The reviewed script structure is readable and does not use encoded payloads, dynamic imports, or hidden execution.

content.value_statement: Teaching with AI can weaken core skills when lessons lack structure. This skill helps educators design balanced programming lessons with AI collaboration and independent verification.
actual_capabilities: Designs programming lessons using AI as teacher, student, and co-worker.
Balances foundational practice, AI-assisted work, and independent verification.
Provides prompt design guidance for debugging, review, explanation, and code generation.
Includes templates for AI-integrated lesson plans and prompt quality checks.
Assesses lesson plans for AI reliance, verification checkpoints, and ethical guidance.
Helps students document AI use and evaluate generated code critically.
limitations: Does not replace an instructor or curriculum designer.
Requires educators to adapt examples to local policies and student level.
Does not verify factual accuracy of external AI responses automatically.
Helper scripts analyze local files only and do not manage a course platform.

use_cases.title: Plan an AI-supported coding lesson
use_cases.description: Create a lesson that introduces a programming concept, uses AI for guided practice, and ends with independent proof of learning.

use_cases.title: Teach prompt quality and verification
use_cases.description: Guide students from vague requests to testable prompts that include context, constraints, expected behavior, and review steps.

use_cases.title: Review AI integration in assignments
use_cases.description: Check whether an assignment protects foundational skills, documents AI use, and prevents passive copying of generated code.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner lesson balance
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a first AI-assisted lesson for new programmers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a 60-minute beginner lesson for [topic]. Include a foundation phase without AI, a guided AI practice phase, and an independent verification task.

prompt_templates.title: Prompt practice activity
prompt_templates.scenario: Teach students how to improve prompts.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an activity where students improve a vague coding prompt for [language]. Include context, task, constraints, expected output, and reflection questions.

prompt_templates.title: AI collaboration workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a spec, generate, validate, and learn cycle.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a lesson segment where students specify intent, ask AI for an approach, critique the result, revise the prompt, and explain what changed.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced curriculum audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate a full assignment for AI over-reliance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this AI-integrated assignment for balance, verification, attribution, ethical use, and independent mastery. Recommend concrete improvements for each weakness.

output_examples.input: A teacher wants a Python functions lesson with limited AI use.
output_examples.output: A phased lesson plan with manual function practice, guided AI explanation prompts, test case generation, and a final no-AI coding check.

output_examples.input: A bootcamp wants students to learn better debugging prompts.
output_examples.output: A classroom activity where students compare weak and strong bug reports, add expected behavior, and verify fixes before using them.

output_examples.input: A curriculum lead asks whether an assignment encourages passive AI use.
output_examples.output: A review that flags missing independent attempts, weak disclosure rules, and absent verification, then suggests assessment changes.

best_practices: Start with independent foundations before students use AI for acceleration.
Require students to test, explain, and document any AI-generated code they use.
Use iterative prompts so students learn from AI feedback and correct it when needed.
anti_patterns: Do not let students copy generated code without reading or testing it.
Do not treat AI as a passive answer machine instead of a learning partner.
Do not skip attribution, disclosure, or local academic integrity rules.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Programming instructors, bootcamp coaches, and curriculum leads who teach students to work responsibly with AI coding tools.

faq.question: Does it create full course curricula?
faq.answer: It supports AI-integrated lesson and assignment design. Educators still need to align content with course goals and policies.

faq.question: Can it help with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is tool-agnostic and applies to common AI coding assistants, including Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: How does it prevent over-reliance on AI?
faq.answer: It recommends foundation phases, no-AI verification tasks, reflection, disclosure, and student explanation of generated code.

faq.question: Are the included scripts required?
faq.answer: No. They are optional local helpers for checking prompt quality and AI integration in lesson files.

faq.question: Does it execute student code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed helper scripts parse lesson or prompt files and print assessments. They do not run submitted code.
